questions.question: Explain the fundamental differences between grid computing and cluster computing in the context of cloud computing. Provide one real-world example for each.
keyPoints: Grid computing integrates heterogeneous resources across geographic locations
Cluster computing uses homogeneous nodes tightly coupled in a local network
Example of grid: LHC Computing Grid for high-energy physics data processing
Example of cluster: Hadoop cluster for large-scale data processing in enterprises

questions.question: Describe the functions of a job scheduler in a distributed computing environment and name two widely used job schedulers in Hadoop and MPI ecosystems.
keyPoints: Job scheduler allocates resources, manages task execution, and handles failures
YARN (Yet Another Resource Negotiator) is the primary scheduler in Hadoop
SLURM and PBS (Portable Batch System) are popular job schedulers for MPI clusters
Schedulers ensure load balancing, data locality, and efficient resource utilization

questions.question: Discuss the MapReduce programming model in detail. Explain the map and reduce phases, and how Hadoop provides fault tolerance and data locality. Provide a real-world example where MapReduce is applied for large-scale data processing.
keyPoints: Map phase processes input key/value pairs into intermediate key/value pairs
Reduce phase aggregates intermediate values per key to produce final output
Hadoop replicates data across nodes (default 3 replicas) for fault tolerance and moves computation to where data resides (data locality)
Real-world example: analyzing web clickstream logs for user behaviour at petabyte scale

questions.question: Explain the Message Passing Interface (MPI) and its role in cluster computing. Compare MPI with Hadoop MapReduce in terms of computational model, communication patterns, and suitability for specific application domains. Give two scientific applications that benefit from MPI.
keyPoints: MPI provides a message-passing library for parallel processes across cluster nodes
Supports point-to-point and collective communication, suited for tightly coupled compute-intensive tasks
MapReduce is batch-oriented, data-intensive, with implicit shuffle communication, while MPI requires explicit message passing
Examples of MPI scientific applications: climate modeling (e.g., Community Earth System Model) and molecular dynamics (e.g., GROMACS)
